summary_sections.title: Understanding Religion: Distributions and Beliefs
summary_sections.content_html: Religion shapes human identity, behavior, cultural practices, and landscapes. Major religions (Christianity, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Judaism) have distinct global distributions reflecting historical patterns of diffusion, conquest, and trade. Religions are monotheistic (one god) or polytheistic (multiple gods), organized hierarchically or decentralized. Religious beliefs influence settlement patterns, agricultural practices, diet, architecture, governance, and social organization.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts and Vocabulary
summary_sections.content_html: Key concepts: religion as cultural and political identity marker; sacred sites concentrating religious significance and pilgrimage; religious diffusion through missionary activity, conquest, and trade; syncretism (blending of religious traditions); fundamentalism (strict adherence to core principles); secularization (declining religious religious adherence, especially in wealthy nations). Religious conflict often expresses underlying territorial, economic, or political tensions more than theological disagreement. Religion intersects with nationalism, ethnicity, and political power.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications and Examples
summary_sections.content_html: Geographic examples: the Middle East where religious identity intersects with national identity and territorial conflict (Israeli-Palestinian conflict); Hindu nationalism in India shaping political identity and spatial policy; Christian fundamentalism influencing US politics and social policy; the geographic distribution of Islam across Africa, Middle East, South Asia, and parts of Southeast Asia; Buddhist cultural landscapes in Thailand and other Southeast Asian nations. Sacred sites like Jerusalem concentrate competing religious claims and geopolitical tension.

summary_sections.title: Deeper Understanding
summary_sections.content_html: Religion shapes cultural landscapes through architecture (temples, mosques, churches), settlement organization, land use practices, and sacred site identification. Religious identity often intertwines with political identity and territorial claims. Understanding religion geographically requires moving beyond belief systems to examine how religion shapes society, politics, and space. Religious variation within traditions (different interpretations and practices) is as important as variation between religions.
